title: Distributed Job Scheduler / Cron — System Design
description: Design a reliable distributed scheduler: run jobs at a scheduled time (one-off or recurring/cron) across a fleet, exactly-once triggered and at-least-once executed, surviving node failures. The themes are durable schedules, efficient due-job lookup, leader election to avoid duplicate triggers, and retries/idempotency.

nodes.label: Client / Service
nodes.description: Registers scheduled or one-off jobs and reads status.

nodes.label: Scheduler API
nodes.description: CRUD for job definitions and schedules; persists durably.

nodes.label: Job / Schedule Store
nodes.description: Authoritative job definitions and schedule state (next_fire_at, version, policies). Also holds the fire-dedup markers, run_outbox, and leader_lease so the fire transaction is one atomic write. It does not own per-run execution state — that lives in RunStore.

nodes.label: Due-Job Index
nodes.description: Time-ordered index of next-fire times so 'what's due now' is cheap. Derived from JobDB's next_fire_at; if it's a separate store (Redis/in-memory) the dispatcher validates each due entry against JobDB before firing and a reconciler rebuilds it.

nodes.label: Dispatcher (Leader)
nodes.description: The elected leader that finds due jobs and enqueues runs exactly once.

nodes.label: Lease / Election Store
nodes.description: Holds the leader lease (fencing token) for the active dispatcher.

nodes.label: Outbox + Relay
nodes.description: Outbox rows written in the fire transaction; a relay publishes them to the run queue at least once.

nodes.label: Run Queue
nodes.description: At-least-once queue of job runs handed to workers.

nodes.label: Job Workers
nodes.description: Execute the job, report success/failure, honor idempotency.

nodes.label: Run / Execution Store
nodes.description: Owns all per-run execution state: run records, status, attempts, claim leases, idempotency key, and result. Workers read/write only here; the dispatcher's overlap check reads active claims from here too.

nodes.label: Retry / Backoff Controller
nodes.description: Reschedules failed runs with backoff; dead-letters after max attempts.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Store
nodes.description: Runs that exhausted retries, for inspection.

nodes.label: Target System
nodes.description: The external endpoint/handler the job acts on. The worker propagates an idempotency key it must honor so a redelivered run causes no double side effect.

links.label: mark fired + advance next-fire + outbox (1 tx)

functional: Register one-off jobs (run at time T) and recurring jobs (cron expression).
Trigger each due job at (approximately) its scheduled time.
Execute the job on a worker; report success/failure and status.
Retry failed runs with backoff; give up after a limit.
nonFunctional: Exactly-once triggering: a scheduled fire produces exactly one run (no duplicates from multiple schedulers).
At-least-once execution with idempotency: a run is retried until it succeeds, and jobs are safe to re-run.
Durable: a registered schedule is never lost; the system survives node crashes.
Scale to millions of schedules and bursts at round times (top of the hour).
Reasonable timing accuracy (seconds), not hard real-time.

capacity.label: Schedules stored
capacity.note: ~1 KB each -> ~10 GB; trivially fits one sharded DB.

capacity.label: Schedule writes
capacity.note: Create/update/cancel; bursty but small vs reads.

capacity.label: Top-of-hour burst
capacity.note: Cron skew to :00. Budget: jitter over 60s -> ~3.3k fires/sec (matches workers); demand all within 5s -> ~40k fires/sec dispatch + far more workers, or accept execution lag while trigger records stay on time.

capacity.label: Worker concurrency
capacity.note: Avg job ~2s -> ~5k jobs/sec; retry amplification ~1.2x -> ~4k effective/sec. Sets the jitter window: a 200k burst drains in ~50s at full pool.

capacity.label: Run retention
capacity.note: ~5k runs/sec * 30d ~= 13B rows; compact/TTL old runs + dedup keys.

api.description: Register a job with a schedule (one-off time or cron). Carries the scheduling policy that defines what 'run at this time' actually means: time zone, validity window, misfire/catch-up, overlap, retry, and timeout. A client_request_id makes create idempotent under client retries.

Update a schedule or pause/resume it. Bumps the schedule version and recomputes next_fire_at from the new expression/timezone in JobDB. If the due index is a column in JobDB the entry is replaced in the same transaction; if it's a separate store the new next_fire_at is propagated via outbox/CDC and a stale old entry is a harmless no-op because the dispatcher validates schedule_version against JobDB before firing. Either way a pending fire keyed on the old (job_id, scheduled_fire, version) can no longer collide with the new schedule.
List recent runs and their status for a job.

Cancel/unschedule a job. Cancellation always stops future fires (state=cancelled, due-index entry removed) and the schedule_version bump invalidates any in-flight fire key. A query/body flag controls already-queued and in-flight runs: by default queued-but-unclaimed runs are marked skipped, while a run already claimed by a worker is left to finish (or, if cancel=hard, the worker is signaled to stop via its lease and the run is marked cancelled on the next heartbeat).

dataModel.name: jobs
dataModel.note: Authoritative job definition + schedule policy + next-fire marker. schedule_version bumps on every update so old pending fires can't collide with a changed schedule.

fields.name: schedule_version
fields.type: int (bumped on update; part of fire identity)

dataModel.name: runs
dataModel.note: Owned by RunStore. Per-execution record; fire identity = (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version). A claim lease + heartbeat lets a dead worker's 'running' claim be reclaimed. The dispatcher reads active (running/queued) rows here to enforce overlap_policy and may insert a 'skipped' row instead of enqueuing.

fields.name: scheduled_fire
fields.type: timestamp (the fire this run is for)

fields.name: attempt_id
fields.type: uuid (this attempt; propagated to target as idempotency key)

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum(queued, running, succeeded, failed, dead, skipped)

fields.name: origin
fields.type: enum(scheduled, manual_rerun) — operator reruns are audited as new runs

fields.name: claimed_by / lease_expires_at
fields.type: worker id + heartbeat lease (reclaim if stale)

dataModel.name: run_outbox
dataModel.note: Lives in JobDB so it's written in the same transaction as the fire marker and next-fire advance; a relay reads it and publishes to the run queue at least once, then marks the row sent. Makes 'mark fired' and 'enqueue run' atomic across separate stores.

dataModel.name: leader_lease
dataModel.note: Election state for the active dispatcher; kept in JobDB (or a coordination store) so the fencing-token check on the fire-marker write is a same-store conditional.

patterns.name: Durable schedule store
patterns.what: Persist job definitions + next-fire so schedules survive restarts.
patterns.whenToUse: Any scheduler that must not forget jobs after a crash.

patterns.name: Time-ordered due index
patterns.what: Index jobs by next-fire time so 'what's due now' is a cheap range scan, not a full scan.
patterns.whenToUse: Many schedules where polling all of them would be too slow.

patterns.name: Leader election + fencing
patterns.what: Elect one dispatcher via a lease with a fencing token so only one triggers each fire.
patterns.whenToUse: Avoiding duplicate triggers from multiple scheduler instances.
patterns.group: Leader Election to Avoid Duplicate Triggers

patterns.name: Exactly-once trigger via dedup
patterns.what: Mark a fire as dispatched (job_id, scheduled_fire) so a re-run of the dispatcher can't double-fire.
patterns.whenToUse: Turning at-least-once dispatch into exactly-once triggering.

patterns.name: At-least-once execution + idempotency
patterns.what: Queue runs at-least-once; workers dedup on a run key so re-delivery is safe.
patterns.whenToUse: Reliable execution where workers can crash mid-run.

patterns.name: Retry with backoff + DLQ
patterns.what: Re-run failures with exponential backoff; dead-letter after a max.
patterns.whenToUse: Transient failures that should self-heal without infinite loops.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Cron Process on a Single Box
description: Start with the simplest scheduler that works: a single process on one machine runs a loop, wakes up every second, scans its in-memory list of jobs for any whose time has come, and fires them. This is essentially `cron` — and for a handful of jobs on one box it's perfectly fine.

It exposes the problems the rest of the design exists to solve. If the box crashes, every in-memory schedule is gone and any job due during the outage simply never runs — the cardinal sin for a scheduler. If you run a second instance for availability, both loops see the same due job and fire it twice. And scanning every job each tick doesn't scale past one machine's memory and CPU.
So the naive loop motivates everything that follows: persist schedules durably, index them for cheap due-lookup, elect a single leader so jobs fire exactly once, and execute reliably with retries.

view.caption: A single cron process holds its schedules in memory and fires them from a loop — no durable store and no second instance, which is exactly what the next steps fix.

steps.decisionPrompt: Your single cron box reboots for 5 minutes. What happens to the jobs that were due during those 5 minutes?
whyNow: Establishing the single-box cron loop first makes every later step's purpose concrete — durability fixes the crash, leader election fixes the double-fire, the due index fixes the full scan.

recap.after: A single process loops, scans jobs in memory, and fires the ones that are due.
recap.newRisk: A crash loses every schedule and silently drops due jobs; a second instance double-fires; scanning all jobs every tick won't scale.

traps.trap: Shipping the single-box cron loop as the design.
traps.why: In-memory schedules vanish on crash, a second instance for HA double-fires every job, and the full scan caps you at one machine.
traps.instead: Persist schedules durably, index them by next-fire time, elect one dispatcher, and execute via an idempotent queue.

steps.title: 2. Durable Schedules
description: First, never forget a job. Persist each job definition and its schedule in a durable store with a computed next_fire_at. The schedule isn't just a cron string: it carries a timezone (so '02:00' is DST-aware), a validity window (start_at/end_at), and policies for the messy real cases — misfire/catch-up (what to do about fires missed during downtime), overlap (whether a new fire may start while the previous run is still going), retry, and timeout. These define what 'run at 02:00' actually means and the later dedup key depends on them.

Give each job a schedule_version, bumped on every update, and make create/update idempotent via a client_request_id. Versioning matters because a fire is identified by (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version): if an update moves a future fire from 02:00 to 03:00, the old pending 02:00 fire key is from a stale version and is ignored, so the change can't double-fire or collide.

Cancel and pause are versioned the same way: bumping the version (and clearing next_fire_at / setting state) stops all future fires and invalidates any pending fire key. What happens to work already in flight is a policy choice the API exposes — stop only future fires, also drop queued-but-unstarted runs, or additionally signal an active worker to abort via its lease. The default is to skip queued runs and let an already-running attempt finish.

The store is the source of truth: a full restart can reconstruct exactly what should run and when. Everything else (the due index, the dispatcher's in-memory view) is derived from and rebuildable from this store.

view.caption: The client registers jobs through the scheduler API, which durably persists each definition to the schedule store.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's the source of truth, and how does the system know what to run after a total restart?

concepts.definition: Persisted job definition with a computed next fire time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Schedules survive crashes; recurring fires are recomputed deterministically.
concepts.example: Cron '0 2 * * *' in America/New_York -> next_fire_at = tomorrow 02:00 local (DST-aware).

concepts.definition: Each job carries a version bumped on update; create/update dedup on a client_request_id.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes the fire key collision-proof across schedule changes and makes client retries safe.
concepts.example: Update v1->v2 reschedules 02:00->03:00; a leftover v1 02:00 fire is ignored.

concepts.definition: Per-job rules for fires missed during downtime (skip / one catch-up / all missed) and for a fire arriving while the prior run still runs (allow / skip / queue one).
concepts.whyItMatters: After an outage or for long-running jobs, these decide correctness, not edge polish.
concepts.example: overlap=skip: the 03:00 fire is skipped because the 02:00 run is still executing.

whyNow: Durability of schedules is the foundation; without it a crash silently drops jobs — the one failure a scheduler can never be allowed to have.

recap.after: All schedules durably persisted with next-fire times.
recap.newRisk: Scanning millions of jobs every tick to find due ones is too slow.

steps.title: 3. Finding Due Jobs Efficiently
description: You can't scan millions of jobs each second to find which are due. Maintain a time-ordered index keyed by next_fire_at so 'what's due in the next window' is a cheap range query (a time wheel / sorted index).

The index is mutable, not append-only: a create adds an entry, a fire removes (or for recurring, re-inserts at the new next_fire_at), and an update/cancel must remove or replace the old entry. Getting these mutations right in the same transaction as the schedule change is what keeps the index from drifting from the store.

Pick what the index physically is, because it changes failover and burst behavior: a durable sorted set (e.g. Redis ZSET keyed on next_fire_at) is fast but needs its own HA; an indexed column (B-tree on next_fire_at) is just the store with no drift; an in-memory shard-local time wheel is fastest but must be rebuilt from the store on takeover.

Consistency depends on which of those you pick, and an interviewer will push here. If the index is a column in JobDB, create/update/cancel/fire mutate the index in the same transaction — it cannot drift. If it's a separate store, you cannot make the JobDB write and the index write atomic, so treat the index as a derived hint, not a source of truth: (1) propagate schedule changes to the index asynchronously via outbox/CDC; (2) make the dispatcher validate lazily — before firing a due entry it re-reads (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version, next_fire_at, state) from JobDB, so a stale entry (job moved/cancelled) is a harmless no-op; (3) run a periodic reconciler that rebuilds/repairs the index from JobDB to recover missing entries (a crash after writing JobDB but before indexing). In all cases the store's next_fire_at is authoritative and the index is rebuildable. The scheduler ticks, pulls the small due set, and fans out round-time bursts.

view.caption: The scheduler API indexes each job's next-fire time in the due-job index, which the leader dispatcher polls for due jobs before marking them fired in the schedule store.

options.name: Time-ordered index / time wheel (default)
options.description: Jobs are bucketed by fire time in a time wheel keyed on next_fire_at, so each tick the dispatcher reads only the imminent bucket as a cheap range query.
pros: 'What's due in the next window' is a cheap range query keyed by next_fire_at, not a scan of millions of jobs.
A time wheel buckets jobs by fire time so the dispatcher only touches the imminent ones each tick.
Derived from and rebuildable from the job store, so a lost index is a recompute, not data loss.
cons: The index must be kept in sync as jobs are added, fired, or rescheduled.
Round-time bursts (everything at 02:00) still land in one bucket and must be fanned out.
Far-future jobs sit in the structure occupying memory or storage until due.

view.caption: The API writes next-fire times into a time-ordered due-job index that the dispatcher polls, marking fired jobs in the schedule store.

options.name: Indexed DB range query (ORDER BY next_fire_at)
options.description: An index on next_fire_at in the job store answers 'due now' directly, with the dispatcher polling the store for due rows each tick.
pros: No separate index structure: an index on next_fire_at in the job store answers 'due now' directly.
One source of truth — the store IS the due index, so nothing can drift out of sync.
Trivial to operate and reason about; survives restart with no rebuild.
cons: Polling the DB every second for due rows adds steady query load at millions of jobs.
Hot range of imminent rows can become a contention/lock hotspot under bursts.
Less control over in-memory bucketing than a purpose-built time wheel.

view.caption: The dispatcher runs an indexed range query over the schedule store (next_fire_at <= now) to find due jobs the API persisted there.

options.name: Periodic full scan (naive)
options.description: Each tick reads every job in the store and checks whether its fire time has arrived, with no index or due-time structure.
pros: Dead simple: each tick, read every job and check if its time has come.
No index to maintain or keep consistent.
Fine for a handful of jobs on one box (this is what cron does).
cons: Scanning millions of jobs every second is hopelessly expensive — the problem this design exists to solve.
CPU and I/O grow with total job count, not with how many are actually due.
Cannot scale past one machine's memory.

view.caption: The dispatcher scans every row in the schedule store each tick to find which jobs are due.

steps.decisionPrompt: With millions of schedules, how do you find the ones due this second without scanning them all?

concepts.definition: Jobs indexed by next-fire time for cheap range lookup of what's due.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns 'find due jobs' into a small range scan instead of a full table scan each tick.
concepts.example: Sorted set keyed by next_fire_at; pop entries <= now.

whyNow: Efficient due lookup is what makes the scheduler scale; the time-ordered index is the standard mechanism and sets up dispatch.

recap.before: Full scan to find due jobs.
recap.after: Cheap range lookup of due jobs each tick.
recap.newRisk: If multiple scheduler instances run, each could fire the same due job — duplicates.

steps.title: 4. Leader Election to Avoid Duplicate Triggers
description: For availability you run multiple scheduler instances — but if they all read the due index and dispatch, every job fires multiple times. Elect a single active dispatcher (per shard) via a lease in a coordination store.

The lease has a TTL and a fencing token; only the lease holder dispatches. If the leader dies, its lease expires and another instance takes over — brief gap, no duplicate leaders. The fencing token guards against a paused-then-resumed old leader acting late: it must be enforced at the write, not just checked in memory — the dispatcher includes its token in the conditional fire-marker write and the store rejects any write carrying a token older than the current one. A stale leader's late fire is rejected even if it still thinks it holds the lease.
Shard the schedule space (by job id hash) across several leader leases so dispatch scales horizontally while each shard still has exactly one active dispatcher.

Make leadership observable: track lease churn (handoffs/min), fencing-token rejections, and the failover gap (time from a leader's last heartbeat to a peer acquiring the lease). A spike in churn or rejects means leaders are flapping — usually a too-short TTL or a slow coordination store — and surfaces as missed-fire lag, so it's worth a dashboard and an alert.

view.caption: The dispatcher acquires a leader lease from the election store before polling the due-job index, so only the leader triggers jobs.

options.name: Lease + fencing token (default)
options.description: A short-TTL lease in a coordination store elects one dispatcher per shard, and a fencing token rejects a stale leader that resumes after pausing.
pros: A short-TTL lease in a coordination store elects one active dispatcher per shard; only the holder dispatches.
On leader death the lease simply expires and a peer takes over — brief gap, no duplicate leaders.
The fencing token rejects a paused-then-resumed old leader acting late.
Sharding the lease space lets dispatch scale horizontally while each shard stays single-writer.
cons: Depends on a coordination store (etcd/ZooKeeper/Redis) that must itself be HA.
A failover gap exists while the lease TTL elapses before takeover.
Clock/TTL tuning matters: too short flaps, too long delays failover.

view.caption: The dispatcher holds a lease from the election store and uses its fencing token while polling the due-job index, preventing a stale leader from firing.

options.name: Consensus-based election (Raft/ZooKeeper)
options.description: A consensus group (Raft/ZooKeeper) elects the active dispatcher with linearizable leadership, so no two leaders dispatch even under partitions.
pros: A consensus group elects a leader with strong safety guarantees — no two leaders even under partitions.
Leadership and committed state are linearizable, removing fencing edge cases.
Battle-tested coordination primitives (sessions, ephemeral nodes) handle liveness.
cons: Heavier to run and operate than a TTL lease for what is essentially mutual exclusion.
A single elected leader per group can become a dispatch bottleneck without sharding.
Election rounds add latency on failover compared with passive lease expiry.

view.caption: A Raft consensus group elects the dispatcher leader, which then polls the due-job index.

options.name: Static partition ownership
options.description: Each dispatcher statically owns a fixed shard of the job-id space, dispatching only its own jobs with no election or lease.
pros: Each dispatcher statically owns a fixed shard of the job-id space — no election at all.
Simple and predictable: no leader, no lease, no failover protocol.
Dispatch scales linearly by adding shards/owners.
cons: When an owner dies, its shard's jobs stop firing until ops reassigns it — availability gap.
Reassignment needs a coordinator anyway, or you risk two owners firing the same shard.
Uneven shards create hot owners with no automatic rebalancing.

view.caption: Each dispatcher statically owns a shard, polling only its slice of the due-job index and marking fired jobs in the schedule store.

steps.decisionPrompt: You run 3 scheduler instances for HA. How do you stop a job from firing 3 times?

concepts.definition: One holder of a TTL lease acts; a fencing token rejects stale ex-leaders.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees a single dispatcher per shard, preventing duplicate triggers, with automatic failover.
concepts.example: Instance A holds lease (token 7); A pauses, B takes over (token 8); A's late write with token 7 is rejected.
concepts.group: Leader Election to Avoid Duplicate Triggers

whyNow: Single-dispatcher election is the classic distributed-cron problem; fencing tokens are the senior detail that makes it actually safe.

recap.after: One leader per shard dispatches; failover without duplicate leaders.
recap.newRisk: Even one leader can crash mid-dispatch and re-dispatch on restart — need fire-level dedup.

traps.trap: Relying on 'only one instance running' by convention.
traps.why: Deploys, network partitions, and restarts inevitably produce two live instances.
traps.instead: Explicit lease-based election with a fencing token.

steps.title: 5. Exactly-Once Triggering
description: Even with one leader, a dispatcher can crash after reading a due job but before recording it, then re-dispatch on restart. Make triggering idempotent: each fire has a natural key (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version). In one transaction the dispatcher marks that key dispatched, advances next_fire_at to the next occurrence, removes/updates the due-index entry, and records the run to publish.

A second attempt to fire the same key is a no-op because it already exists. This turns at-least-once dispatch into exactly-once triggering — one run per scheduled fire. Including schedule_version in the key means a fire computed before an update can't collide with the new schedule.

The dispatcher — not the worker — advances next_fire_at when it claims the fire, so the next occurrence is scheduled independently of whether this run later succeeds (cron semantics, not fixed-delay). Because the run queue is a separate store, the atomic answer is a transactional outbox: commit the fire marker, next_fire advance, and an outbox row together, then a relay publishes to the queue at least once. The 'atomic CAS' shortcut only works if the queue is the same transactional store as the marker.

This is also where the Step 2 policies stop being mere fields and start shaping the transaction. The per-fire dispatch algorithm: (1) load the job row and, if the due index is a separate store, validate the entry against schedule_version/state; (2) compute which fire keys are due by misfire_policy — `skip` advances next_fire_at past missed fires without enqueuing, `run_one_catchup` enqueues one representative missed fire and advances past the rest, `run_all_missed` enumerates every missed key (bounded, so a long outage doesn't explode); (3) for each candidate fire, apply overlap_policy by reading active run state from RunStore — `allow` always enqueues, `skip` records a 'skipped' run instead of enqueuing when a prior run is still active, `queue_one` enqueues at most one waiter; (4) in one transaction insert the run rows as queued or skipped, advance next_fire_at, update/remove the due entry, and write outbox rows only for the queued runs. So misfires and overlaps change which keys are inserted, skipped, or queued — not just metadata.

view.caption: The leader dispatcher marks the fire and writes an outbox row in one transaction; a relay moves it onto the run queue, so the mark and the enqueue can't diverge across a crash.

options.name: Transactional outbox (default)
options.description: The fire marker, the next_fire advance, and an outbox row commit in one DB transaction; a relay reads the outbox and publishes the run to the queue at least once. This is the production default whenever the run queue is a separate store from the schedule DB.
pros: Mark-fired, next_fire advance, and the outbox row commit in one DB transaction, so the enqueue can never be lost or doubled even if the dispatcher crashes mid-step.
A relay reads the outbox and publishes to the run queue, retrying safely until acked.
Decouples 'decided to fire' (durable) from 'delivered to queue' (at-least-once) cleanly — no crash window where the mark and the enqueue disagree.
cons: Adds an outbox table and a relay process to build and operate.
The relay's at-least-once publish still needs consumer-side idempotency downstream (the run key handles this).
Slight added latency between marking fired and the run appearing on the queue.

view.caption: The dispatcher marks the job fired, advances next_fire, and writes an outbox row in the same transaction; a relay then moves it from the outbox into the run queue.

options.name: Atomic mark + enqueue (CAS)
options.description: Keyed on (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version), the dispatcher atomically marks the fire done and enqueues the run in one write — only sound when the queue lives in the same transactional store as the marker (e.g. a DB-backed queue), so the two writes share a transaction.
pros: A natural fire key plus a single atomic mark-and-enqueue makes re-dispatch a no-op — no separate relay to run.
Turns at-least-once dispatch into exactly-once triggering with the least moving parts when the queue is already in the DB.
Lowest latency: the run is visible to workers the moment the fire commits.
cons: Only correct if the marker and the run-queue write are in one transactional store; a normal DB plus a normal queue (Kafka/SQS) cannot be made atomic this way — that's what the outbox is for.
The fire-dedup keys must be retained long enough to catch late retries.
Couples the run queue to the schedule DB's throughput and scaling.

view.caption: When the run queue is the same transactional store, the dispatcher marks the fire and enqueues the run in one write, so a duplicate trigger can't slip through.

steps.decisionPrompt: The dispatcher enqueues a run, then crashes before recording it fired. On restart it sees the job still due. What stops a double-fire?

concepts.definition: Dedup a fire on (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version) so re-dispatch can't duplicate it and a changed schedule can't collide with an old pending fire.
concepts.whyItMatters: Combines with leader election to guarantee one run per scheduled occurrence; the version guards updates/cancels.
concepts.example: Unique constraint on (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version); a repeat insert is ignored.

whyNow: Fire-level dedup is what actually delivers exactly-once triggering; election alone isn't enough, and naming this is the senior signal.

recap.after: Each scheduled fire produces exactly one run.
recap.newRisk: The run still has to execute reliably on a worker that may itself fail.

flows.name: Poll due -> policy check -> dedup tx -> relay enqueue
flows.note: The leader pulls due jobs; for each it validates the entry and applies misfire/overlap policy (reading active runs from RunStore), then in one transaction marks the fire, advances next_fire, inserts the run as queued or skipped, and writes an outbox row only for queued runs. A relay publishes queued runs to the queue at least once. A re-dispatch after a crash hits the existing fire key and no-ops.

messages.label: tx: mark fired + advance next_fire + queue run + outbox row
record skipped run + advance next_fire (no outbox)

steps.title: 6. At-Least-Once Execution with Idempotency
description: Dispatched runs go onto an at-least-once queue consumed by stateless workers. A worker claims a run with a lease, executes the job, and records the outcome. At-least-once means a run can be delivered twice (worker crash before ack), so execution must be idempotent. The schedule was already advanced by the dispatcher in Step 5, so a slow or failed run never blocks the next occurrence — workers only write run status.

There are two distinct layers of idempotency. Infrastructure idempotency: the worker claims the fire key in the run store before executing, so two workers can't both run the same delivery and a redelivery to a finished run is skipped. Target idempotency: that claim alone does not stop duplicate external side effects if the worker crashes after performing the action but before recording success — so the worker propagates an idempotency key (attempt_id / fire key) to the target, and the target must honor it (or the side effect must be transactional with the run store).

A claim is a lease with a heartbeat and a timeout: if the worker dies mid-run, the lease expires and the run is reclaimed and redelivered, where the target idempotency key prevents a double side effect. A run stuck 'running' past its timeout is reclaimed the same way.

It helps to name the run state machine, because it ties together claims, retries, DLQ, overlap policy, and the status API: `queued -> running` (worker claims a lease) -> `succeeded` | `failed` | `dead` (retries exhausted); `running -> queued` on lease expiry (reclaim and redeliver); and `queued -> skipped` when overlap_policy suppresses a fire that was never started. Every status API and metric reads off this machine.

view.caption: The run queue delivers runs to job workers, which claim a lease in the run store and act on the target with a propagated idempotency key; a stale lease (dead worker) is reclaimed and redelivered, and the target's key absorbs the duplicate.

steps.decisionPrompt: A worker performs the job's side effect, then crashes before recording success. The run redelivers. What actually stops a double side effect?

concepts.definition: Reliable delivery that may duplicate, made safe at two layers: a run-store claim (infra) plus an idempotency key the target honors (side effect).
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees the job runs without relying on impossible exactly-once delivery; the target key, not just the claim, is what makes the external side effect safe.
concepts.example: Run keyed (j_9, 02:00, v1); the worker sends idempotency-key j_9:02:00 to the target, so a redelivered copy is a no-op end-to-end.

concepts.definition: A worker holds a time-bounded lease on a run, renewed by heartbeat; a stale lease is reclaimed.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets a dead or hung worker's 'running' claim be safely redelivered instead of stranding the run forever.
concepts.example: Worker claims run with a 60s lease, heartbeats every 20s; it crashes, the lease expires, another worker picks it up.

whyNow: Reliable execution is the other half of correctness; pairing at-least-once delivery with idempotency is the standard, honest answer (exactly-once delivery is a myth).

recap.before: A worker crash could lose or duplicate execution.
recap.after: Runs execute at-least-once; a claim lease plus a target-honored idempotency key make them effectively-once even across a crash mid-side-effect.
recap.newRisk: Jobs fail for transient reasons and must be retried sensibly, not forever.

flows.name: Claim lease -> execute with target idempotency
flows.note: A worker claims a run with a lease, executes while propagating the idempotency key to the target, and records success. A redelivery after a crash is recognized by the target's idempotency key even if the run-store success write was lost.

messages.label: claim lease (fire key); skip if already done
idempotency-key seen -> no double side effect

steps.title: 7. Retries, Backoff, and Dead-Lettering
description: Transient failures (a downstream blip, a timeout) should self-heal: on failure the retry controller re-enqueues the run with exponential backoff and jitter, incrementing the attempt count in the run store. The bounds come from the job's own retry_policy (max_attempts, backoff, base), not a global constant, so a flaky integration and a critical batch can retry differently.

Classify errors: retry transient errors (5xx, timeout, connection reset) but fail fast on non-retryable ones (4xx validation, auth, malformed target) — retrying those just burns attempts. After max_attempts (or on a non-retryable error) stop and move the run to a dead-letter store, then alert the job's owner so a permanently-broken job doesn't retry forever or hide.

DLQ is not the end of the workflow: an operator inspects the dead-lettered run, fixes the cause, and either reruns it or marks it abandoned. The rerun has two flavors with different identity: an idempotent retry of the same scheduled fire re-enqueues the original fire key (so it stays deduped against any other delivery), whereas a deliberate fresh execution creates a new run with origin=manual_rerun and a new idempotency key (so the target treats it as a new side effect, not a replay). Either way the operator action — who, when, which run, leave/abandon/rerun — is recorded for audit. Combined with the earlier guarantees — trigger once, execute at-least-once with idempotency, retry with policy-bounded backoff, dead-letter and remediate on give-up — this is a complete reliable-execution lifecycle.

view.caption: Workers report failures to the retry controller, which re-enqueues the run with backoff onto the run queue or gives up to the dead-letter store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A job keeps failing. How do you retry transient errors without retrying a broken job forever?

concepts.definition: Per-job policy-bounded exponential-backoff retries on transient errors, fail-fast on non-retryable ones, then route to a DLQ with owner alerting and operator rerun.
concepts.whyItMatters: Self-heals transient failures while bounding effort on permanent ones and giving a clear remediation path instead of silent loss.
concepts.example: Retry timeouts at 1m, 2m, 4m...; after 6 attempts -> dead-letter + alert owner -> operator reruns by fire key.

whyNow: Closing on retry/backoff/DLQ completes the reliable-execution story and shows you handle permanent failure gracefully.

recap.after: Backoff retries + dead-letter; full reliable lifecycle.
recap.newRisk: Round-time fire bursts and leader failover gaps stress the system — bounded by sharding and short lease TTLs.

bottlenecks.issue: Thundering herd of jobs at the top of the hour.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Spread/jitter fire times; the run queue + worker autoscale absorb the burst.

bottlenecks.issue: Leader failover leaves a brief dispatch gap.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Short lease TTL; due index means a slightly-late leader still finds and fires overdue jobs (caught up, deduped).

failureDrills.scenario: The leader dispatcher crashes at 02:00 during a fire burst.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: A new leader takes the lease, reads still-due jobs from the index, and fires them (dedup prevents doubles); jobs fire slightly late, not never.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lease failover + durable due index + exactly-once trigger keys.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Distributed Scheduler
finalDesign.description: JobDB is the source of truth: it owns job definitions, schedule state, fire-dedup markers, the run_outbox, and the leader_lease; RunStore separately owns per-run execution state (status, attempts, claim leases, results). Schedules persist durably with a computed next-fire time, a timezone, a schedule_version, and misfire/overlap/retry/timeout policies, and are indexed by time (the due index) for cheap due-job lookup — if that index is a separate store, the dispatcher validates each entry against JobDB before firing and a reconciler rebuilds it. Per shard, a single dispatcher is elected via a TTL lease with a fencing token enforced at the fire-marker write; for each due fire it applies misfire/overlap policy (reading active runs from RunStore), then in one JobDB transaction dedups on (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version), advances next_fire_at, inserts the run queued or skipped, and writes an outbox row — a relay then publishes queued runs to the queue at least once, giving exactly-once triggering even across crashes/failover. Idempotent workers claim runs with a lease, execute while propagating an idempotency key the target honors, and record outcomes in RunStore. A retry controller re-enqueues transient failures with policy-bounded backoff and dead-letters the rest for owner alerting and operator rerun. Round-time bursts are smoothed by jitter and absorbed by the queue + autoscaled workers.

view.caption: The client registers jobs via the API into the schedule store (JobDB) and due-job index; a leased leader dispatcher polls due jobs, checks active runs in RunStore for overlap policy, marks each fire and writes an outbox row in one JobDB transaction, and a relay enqueues runs that workers execute against the target (with an idempotency key) and record in RunStore, with failures retried through backoff or dead-lettered.

functional.how: Durable schedule with cron/one-off + recomputed next-fire.
Time-ordered due index polled by the leader.
Run queue + idempotent workers + run store.
Versioned update of the durable definition + due-index entry replaced/invalidated; old fire keys ignored.

nonFunctional.how: Leader election + (job_id, scheduled_fire, schedule_version) dedup committed with next-fire + outbox in one transaction.
At-least-once queue + claim lease + idempotency key honored by the target.
Durable schedules + rebuildable due index + lease failover.

technologyChoices.concern: API front door, auth, and tenant quotas
tradeoff: The scheduler API is a multi-tenant control plane, so auth, audit context, and write quotas should be enforced before writes hit JobDB.
A managed API front door reduces custom OAuth, rate limiting, and WAF code, but provider quota models can shape tenant-level throttling behavior.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A gateway with built-in auth and usage plans removes much of the custom per-route quota and token-validation middleware.

tradeoff: Use a managed scheduler when jobs are mostly service invocations and provider limits match the required scale, latency, and tenancy model.
Build the distributed scheduler when you need custom overlap/misfire policy, massive tenant isolation, exact fire-key semantics, or provider-neutral execution.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed scheduler can eliminate the custom due index, leader election, and dispatcher for simple scheduled invocations.

technologyChoices.concern: Schedule / job store and fire marker
tradeoff: A transactional SQL store makes fire marker, next_fire_at advance, and outbox row one atomic write, which is the cleanest exactly-once trigger path.
A horizontally scaling KV or globally distributed SQL store handles very high write volume, but it can make range scans and outbox transactions less natural.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A same-store DB-backed run queue can make the separate outbox relay unnecessary, but it couples queue throughput to the schedule store.

tradeoff: An indexed next_fire_at column never drifts from the source of truth, but steady polling and hot imminent ranges can pressure the DB.
A Redis-style sorted set or time bucket index is fast under bursts, but it is derived state and must tolerate stale and missing entries.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: If the due index is just a JobDB index, the API does not need a separate index propagation path or rebuild job.

technologyChoices.concern: Outbox relay / CDC for derived state
tradeoff: An outbox relay is simple to reason about and gives explicit retry/DLQ behavior for run-queue publishing and due-index updates.
CDC reduces custom polling and can feed multiple derived consumers, but lag and schema drift become production concerns.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A same-transaction due index and DB-backed queue remove most of the outbox/CDC plumbing.

tradeoff: Consensus stores such as etcd or ZooKeeper give strong leadership safety, but they add a coordination dependency to operate.
A conditional-write lease in the same DB used by the dispatcher keeps the stack smaller, but TTL and clock-skew tuning become part of correctness.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: If the due index is sharded by static ownership and accepts manual failover gaps, the active leader-election service can be simplified.

technologyChoices.concern: Run queue and delayed execution buffer
tradeoff: A work queue gives visibility timeout, ack/retry, and DLQ semantics that fit at-least-once worker execution.
A stream/log supports replay and fanout but makes per-run claiming, backoff, and delay scheduling more application-owned.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A queue with native delay, visibility timeout, and DLQ support removes custom retry timers for many failed-run cases.

technologyChoices.concern: Run / execution state, idempotency, and retention
tradeoff: A SQL run store gives clear status queries and transactional overlap checks, but billions of retained runs need partitioning and compaction.
A KV or wide-column store scales idempotency keys and TTL retention cheaply, but rich per-job history queries need secondary indexes or projections.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Native TTL on the run/idempotency store removes a custom cleanup job for expired dedup windows and old attempts.

tradeoff: Serverless and batch runtimes absorb top-of-hour bursts with little idle cost, but duration, concurrency, cold-start, and networking limits shape job design.
A managed cluster handles long-running jobs and custom runtimes, but autoscaling and bin-packing become scheduler-adjacent operational work.
Workflow engines make bounded retries, timers, and operator-visible state explicit, but can overlap with scheduler logic if every run is already modeled in RunStore.
Queue-native retry and DLQ behavior is simpler for short jobs, but complex retry policies and manual reruns require more application code.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A workflow engine with durable timers can replace a separate retry controller for long-running or multi-step jobs.

tradeoff: Track schedule lag, fire-to-enqueue latency, queue backlog, worker claim latency, duplicate-suppression count, lease churn, retry rate, DLQ rate, and missed-fire count.
Auditing create, update, pause, cancel, manual rerun, and DLQ actions is part of correctness in a multi-tenant scheduler, not just compliance.

say: Confirm one-off + cron, ~seconds accuracy (not hard real-time).
Pin exactly-once triggering and at-least-once-with-idempotency execution.
Establish durability and crash-survival as hard requirements.
Surface schedule semantics early: timezone/DST, misfire/catch-up after downtime, and overlap policy for long runs.
Time-ordered due index for cheap lookup.
A leader dispatcher enqueues runs to workers.
Leader election + fencing (token enforced at the fire-marker write).
Exactly-once trigger via (job, fire, version) dedup + next-fire advance + outbox in one transaction.
At-least-once execution + claim lease + target-honored idempotency; retries/backoff/DLQ with rerun.
Tradeoffs: lease TTL vs failover gap, exactly-once trigger vs at-least-once execute, round-time bursts.
Mention job dependencies/DAGs and priorities as extensions.

expectations: Persists schedules and polls for due jobs.
Runs jobs on workers with retries.
May miss leader election and exactly-once triggering.
Exactly-once trigger dedup + at-least-once idempotent execution.
Reasons about failover gaps, fencing correctness, and round-time bursts.
Distinguishes exactly-once trigger from at-least-once execute precisely.

followUps: Why is exactly-once delivery impossible, and how do you get effectively-once execution anyway?
How short should the leader lease TTL be, and what's the failover tradeoff?
How would you handle job dependencies / DAGs (run B after A)?
How do you smooth a thundering herd of jobs all scheduled for the top of the hour?
How would you add per-job priorities or rate limits?

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: Large-scale Cluster Management at Google with Borg
links.why: Shows how a production scheduler handles scale, quotas, priorities, availability, and operational tooling.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: Omega: Flexible, Scalable Schedulers for Large Compute Clusters
links.why: Explains shared-state scheduling and optimistic concurrency tradeoffs that matter when sharding dispatcher ownership.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: Making Time for Cron Triggers: A Look Inside
links.why: Describes a shipped edge cron system with database-backed schedules, schedule distribution, and runtime triggering.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: FOQS: Scaling a Distributed Priority Queue
links.why: Gives production details for delayed enqueue, ack/nack, redelivery, multitenancy, and large backlogs of deferred work.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: Jupiter: A High-Performance Job-Matching Service
links.why: Shows how worker capability matching, queue ownership, reliability, and atomic job assignment shape execution design.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: Twine: Efficient, Reliable Cluster Management
links.why: Useful for sharded scheduler control planes, failover, regional/global ownership, and scheduler-vs-allocator separation.

links.groupDescription: Real production schedulers, job dispatchers, and large-scale scheduling papers.
links.title: User Engagement Powered by Apache Pig and Hadoop
links.why: Shows Azkaban used as a workflow scheduler for dependent batch jobs, a useful comparison for DAG follow-ups.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Scheduler
links.why: Explains the scheduler loop, metadata DB pressure, and row-level locking used to keep scheduling decisions consistent.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Timetables
links.why: Clarifies cron schedules, data intervals, trigger times, and catch-up behavior for durable schedule modeling.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: CronJob
links.why: Documents concurrencyPolicy, starting deadlines, missed schedules, and at-least-once CronJob creation semantics.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: JDBC JobStore Clustering
links.why: Concrete reference for clustered schedulers where exactly one node fires each trigger occurrence.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: CronTrigger Tutorial
links.why: Defines misfire instructions such as fire-now and do-nothing, matching the dataset's downtime policy choices.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Amazon EventBridge Scheduler
links.why: Shows managed one-time and recurring schedules with flexible windows, retry limits, event age, and DLQ configuration.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: About Cloud Scheduler
links.why: States at-least-once delivery and idempotent-target expectations for managed cron jobs.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Retry Jobs
links.why: Details exponential backoff retry behavior and how retries can interact with the next scheduled execution.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Timer Trigger for Azure Functions
links.why: Documents schedule triggers and the storage lock used to keep only one timer instance active when scaled out.

links.groupDescription: Scheduler documentation that exposes real semantics for catch-up, overlap, retries, locks, and limits.
links.title: Temporal Schedule API
links.why: Defines overlap policies, catch-up windows, backfill, pause, and scheduled action state for durable workflow schedules.

links.groupDescription: References for leases, fencing, leader election, and atomic publish-after-write patterns.
links.title: Leases
links.why: Shows how a production control plane represents leases for heartbeats and component leader election.

links.groupDescription: References for leases, fencing, leader election, and atomic publish-after-write patterns.
links.title: How to Conduct Leader Election in etcd Cluster
links.why: Official leader-election reference for a consensus-backed coordination store.

links.groupDescription: References for leases, fencing, leader election, and atomic publish-after-write patterns.
links.title: How to Do Distributed Locking
links.why: Explains why a scheduler lease needs fencing tokens checked by the protected store, not just a lock holder flag.

links.groupDescription: References for leases, fencing, leader election, and atomic publish-after-write patterns.
links.title: Outbox Event Router
links.why: Concrete implementation reference for transactional outbox events that avoid split-brain between DB state and queue publishing.

links.groupDescription: Queue delivery, worker retry, dead-letter, and idempotency references.

links.why: Concrete reference for worker claim leases, heartbeat extension, redelivery, and at-least-once queue execution.

links.groupDescription: Queue delivery, worker retry, dead-letter, and idempotency references.

links.groupDescription: Queue delivery, worker retry, dead-letter, and idempotency references.

links.groupDescription: Queue delivery, worker retry, dead-letter, and idempotency references.

links.groupDescription: Monitoring and telemetry references for scheduler SLOs and incident response.

links.why: Useful baseline for instrumenting scheduler traces, metrics, and logs across API, dispatcher, queue, and workers.

links.groupDescription: Monitoring and telemetry references for scheduler SLOs and incident response.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
